reasoning: The base rate of an IRAT emergence score exceeding 8.0 is mathematically unprecedented—no virus has ever scored that high, and the highest on record was an H7N9 strain at ~6.5-7.5 range. Current H5N1 scores from 2024-2025 (such as A/California/147/2024) are hovering in the moderate range around 5.2-5.6. Achieving a score of 8.0+ would require major shifts in high-weighted categories like human-to-human transmission (currently zero sustained), airborne transmission in ferrets (currently highly inefficient), and human receptor binding preference (still strongly avian). With only ~50 days remaining before the January 1, 2026 resolution date, and considering the significant lag time (often several months) for the CDC to convene experts and publish an updated IRAT score, it is virtually impossible for a new score of ≥ 8.0 to be evaluated, completed, and published before the end of the year unless a severe and widespread human-to-human transmission crisis was already in full swing and publicly documented.
